data.title: Super Micro Computer: I Am Buying The Dip On The Earnings "Miss" That Wasn't One
data.description:
The market just handed investors a gift when Super Micro (NASDAQ:SMCI) tanked 20% following Q4 earnings that showed a slight revenue miss against aggressive expectations.

SMCI is not just a traditional server vendor anymore it is turning into a full stack AI infrastructure provider through Data Center Building Block Solutions DCBBS and next-generation liquid cooling technology.

SMCI just reported 47% annual revenue growth and is ramping production of liquid cooling systems that solve one of AI's biggest bottlenecks, the inability to cool chips that are consuming up to 2700 watts of power. They're shipping complete data centers in weeks instead of the industry standard 12-18 months.

The market is pricing SMCI like Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL) or Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE) server companies growing at 8-11% annually. Yet SMCI is projecting 61% forward revenue growth and trading at a PEG ratio that's 35% below the sector median. The technology moat they're building with their DLC-2 cooling systems and the massive sovereign AI opportunity in Europe and the Middle East, I believe that 20% drop is a solid buying opportunity.

The Earnings "Miss" That Wasn't Really a Miss

Let's cut through the narrative and see what actually happened. SMCI reported Q4 revenue of $5.76 billion against expectations of about $6.2 billion, a miss of roughly 7%. But here's what the market completely overlooked: this "disappointing" quarter still delivered 8% y/y growth and 25% q/q growth. More importantly, their FY25 revenue reached $22 billion which is 47% annual growth.

AI platforms made up over 70% of Q4 revenues across both enterprise and cloud service provider markets, showing SMCI's successful positioning in the AI infrastructure buildout.

This revenue composition tells me that SMCI is well positioned to capture a solid share of the fastest growing segment in enterprise infrastructure. And I am not talking about just the US, I am talking globally. If you look at geographic revenue distribution the US revenues was only 38% of Q4 revenues and decreased 21% QoQ, this was more than offset by growth in international markets. Revenue from Asia grew 78% QoQ and was 42% of revenue, and surprisingly Europe is finally catching up in AI infrastructure spending because revenue from Europe grew 196%.

What caused the earnings volatility wasn't operational weakness but timing shifts from major customers and the impact of delayed 10-K filings that temporarily constrained revenue recognition. On that earnings call, CFO David Weigand said that "large customer orders are now slated for recognition in September and December quarters" following close collaboration to align with updated customer requirements.

The capital constraints were directly tied to the delayed filing of their FY24 10K which created a six month cash flow impact that limited their ability to scale production. This wasn't a fundamental business problem, it was an administrative issue that had been resolved already. Large customer orders that had been delayed are now positioned for recognition in the September and December quarters setting up potential for improved performance in the coming periods.

The specification changes from major customers that impacted production are actually reflecting something positive about their business because it tells me that they were working so closely with large customers on these deployments that these customers were requesting additional features. This should strengthens competitive positioning rather than weakening it, yet the market interpreted the resulting revenue timing delay as a negative indicator.

As you can see in this quarter, their cash flow has also started to improve from the impact of delayed filings. Their Cash flow from operations came in at $864 million in Q4, up from $237 million in the last quarter.

The gross margin performance that scared some investors is actually because of the strategy that SMCI is executing Q4 non-GAAP gross margin of 9.6% was slightly below Q3's 9.7%, but this compression was entirely because of the change in their business model toward higher volume, hyperscaler customers.

CFO David Weigand said on the call that the margin performance is mainly because of the product and customer mix rather than pricing pressure or cost inflation. Their shift toward OEM appliances and large data center customers, which are now 63% of revenue versus 57% in the previous quarter, naturally carried a different margin than traditional enterprise channel sales.

DCBBS: A Strategic Approach To AI Infrastructure

The market's focus on quarterly beats and misses completely ignores the most significant strategic pivot in SMCI's history: the launch and rapid scaling of Data Center Building Block Solutions (DCBBS). I think this is a fundamental business model change that positions SMCI to capture exponentially more value from the AI infrastructure buildout.

Traditional data center deployments require 12-18 months from planning to operation. In the age of AI model development cycles are measured in weeks and this timeline simply cannot keep pace with the velocity of AI model development and deployment requirements. DCBBS addresses this bottleneck by delivering what Charles Liang said as complete data center solutions "in a matter of months or even weeks, rather than many quarters or years."

DCBBS delivers up to 30% lower total cost of ownership while reducing deployment timelines by 75 to 80%. Every month of deployment delay is millions in opportunity costs as these companies race for AI market dominance. If SMCI can compress 18-month buildouts into 3-6 month deployments while reducing costs then the value proposition is simply the best.

But what sets apart DCBBS from existing solutions like Dell's APEX or Hewlett Packard's GreenLake is the ownership model. Unlike consumption based "as-a-Service" approaches where vendors retain control, DCBBS allows customers to own and operate their infrastructure while using SMCI's integration expertise. This matters enormously for AI workloads where data sovereignty and operational control are absolutely necessary.

Their DCBBS strategy is already gaining traction because their server and storage systems made up 98% of the Q4 revenue and management also indicated that they are positioning for higher margin DCBBS solutions. Full stack solutions usually command 20 to 30% premium margins compared to commodity server sales and the service components create recurring revenue streams, which would provide them cushion from the cyclicality of the Data center infrastructure market.

Management expects DCBBS to ramp up starting in Q1 FY26 with much higher volume expected in Q2. They've already begun deploying rack scale solutions so I think that they may deliver ahead of schedule on this.

DLC-2 Moat That Can't Be Replicated

Perhaps even more strategically significant than DCBBS is SMCI's second-generation Direct Liquid Cooling DLC-2 technology. Which is absolutely essential as AI workloads scale to power densities that make traditional air cooling physically impossible.

Nvidia's Blackwell architecture and new AI chips consume somewhere between 1200 to 2700 watts. I am talking about just one GPU consuming this much energy. So, the normal air cooling approaches simply cannot support the rack densities for large scale AI training and inference.

SMCI's DLC-2 addresses this problem by delivering a 40% reduction in power and water consumption compared to traditional cooling methods, while operating at approximately 50 decibels which is quiet enough for deployment in environments where traditional server farms would be prohibitive.

The market opportunity is huge and growing faster than anticipated. The direct to chip liquid cooling market is projected to grow to $24.8 billion by 2034 growing at a CAGR 25.8%.

But these projections actually understate SMCI's opportunity because they focus on market size rather than market timing. The thing is that this transition is happening now, not gradually over the next decade.

Dell and Hewlett Packard, despite their scale advantages have historically focused on air cooled solutions optimized for general purpose computing workloads. They're now scrambling to catch up, but it requires manufacturing capabilities and operational expertise that takes years to develop.

I think that they are a year or two behind SMCI which gives SMCI a first mover advantage in this space. SMCI is already producing 2,000 DLC racks per month with capacity for 250kW and 500kW configurations. They can ship 50MW facilities within three months. They have already done it for customers like xAI, CoreWeave and other major AI hyperscalers.

Sovereign AI Opportunity

One of the most underappreciated aspects of SMCI's growth trajectory is the emerging sovereign AI opportunity. European governments and Middle Eastern sovereign wealth funds are planning massive AI infrastructure investments, but they require partners who can deliver complete solutions. On the earnings call, Liang highlighted that "in sovereign AI, it brings us a very good chance...we see a very good room, very big room to grow in that area." CFO Weigand said that they're "optimistic that we will be able to sell more complete data center BBS solutions with sovereigns."

This would be a massive addressable market expansion. Sovereign AI initiatives typically involve multi-billion dollar commitments unlike hyperscaler deployments which are concentrated in a few major markets, sovereign AI would create geographic diversification that reduces concentration risk and also opens entirely new revenue streams. The urgency around national AI means these projects would have accelerated timelines and premium pricing which is exactly the type of opportunities where SMCI's DCBBS approach delivers maximum value.

Undervaluation

I think that the current valuation is misunderstood by the market because SMCI's is not a cyclical server manufacturer rather than an AI infrastructure provider.

It is also performing way better in this space than its peers the forward revenue growth expectations for SMCI are 61% versus 8% for Dell and 11% for Hewlett Packard which tells us the fundamental difference in market positioning.

Yes, SMCI's forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 17.8x looks elevated compared to traditional server competitors like Hewlett Packard at 10.8x and Dell at 13.6x, and HP, Inc (HPQ) at just 8x. But this comparison misses the fundamental difference in business models and growth trajectories.

The PEG ratio tells the real story here. At 0.7, SMCI trades at a significant discount to the sector median of 1.8 despite projecting over 60% forward revenue growth compared to the sector median of just 7.4%. This is the fundamental difference in the business model that the market is completely failing to price appropriately.

This undervaluation is just not limited to the growth lens their forward P/S ratio of 1.26x is massively discounted by about 58% to the sector median of 3x. The market is pricing SMCI as a low growth server assembler rather than a full-stack AI infrastructure provider which is capturing a solid share of the fastest growing segment in enterprise technology.

What Could Go Wrong

The major risk for them and my bull case is that their growth is increasingly dependent on large hyperscaler deployments which can create revenue lumpiness and margin pressure. Aggressive competition and large customer pricing power continue to pressure gross margins which declined from 13.9% in FY24 to 11.2% in FY25.

SMCI maintains their technological lead in liquid cooling and integrated solutions but this advantage could erode more quickly than I anticipate. Because Dell and HPE aren't going to leave the AI infrastructure market without a fight and they have deeper pockets too that could enable them to catch up faster than the 12-18 months I've estimated.

Final Thoughts

The market handed us a classic case of short-term panic creating long term opportunity. I believe the market is underestimating the value of being a year ahead in liquid cooling technology and the ability to deploy complete AI data centers in weeks rather than years.

data.title: Paycom: Well Positioned And Highly Competitive, But Growth Prospects Are Thinning
data.description:
Four months after my initial coverage, Paycom Software, Inc. (NYSE:PAYC) maintained a robust performance with its double-digit revenue growth and stable margins. Its Q2 2025 performance highlighted its strong market and financial positioning. However, it may have a hard time speeding up its revenue growth back to its 2020-2022 highs as return-to-office orders begin. This is why I believe a stock price upside may be hard to achieve soon despite its cheapness relative to previous years. Technicals adhere to our view as early bearish signals appear.

PAYC Q2 2025: Growth And Margins Are Synchronized

As the digital and AI revolution continues amid the shifting working setup preferences, human capital management or HCM solution providers see new opportunities to sustain their demand today. Paycom Software, Inc. continues to reap the rewards of these market trends considering its established reputation, huge market presence, and well-diversified solution and service offerings. These are reflected in its most recent performance.

Its operating revenue amounted to $483.6M, up by 10.5% YoY from $437.5M. Its recurring revenue remains the primary growth driver, comprising 94% of the total revenue. It rose by 12.2% YoY, which shows PAYC’s sustained demand and high client retention. It is also worth noting that its revenue growth is a slight rebound from its weakening trend since 2024. We can say that HCM solutions may have already peaked or saturated their potential for growth and expansion.

But right now, what’s important is how each player ensures a solid customer base so as not to lose revenues. That’s what we are seeing in PAYC. The improved revenue growth this quarter not only shows its customer loyalty but also its ability to capture new demand amid increased competition with key competitors like ADP, Inc. (ADP), Paychex (PAYX), Paylocity (PCTY), and Dayforce (DAY).

Moreover, PAYC ensures high efficiency despite increasing its operating capacity. As we can see, its operating costs only increased by 4.3%, which is less than half its revenue growth. This is a crucial aspect considering the stubborn inflation in the U.S. In addition, its operating leverage remained stable and decreased from 24.5% to 23.6%. This shows lower cost sensitivity amid market volatility, making it even more impressive. Likewise, the increase in operating expenses of 9.6% is still lower than revenue growth. Hence, it’s not surprising that the operating margin improved from 21.7% to 23.2%.

Positives

PAYC’s enhanced efficiency and established reputation can help preserve its competitive advantage. Its increasing margins show it can expand and catch up with the continued digital revolution while stabilizing R&D and tech infrastructure expenses. This offers it some flexibility in price adjustments to remain highly competitive or in-demand without sacrificing profits. In addition, its recurring revenue model provides it with some insulation and resilience as this means secure revenue streams for a specific period as agreed upon. As we can see, PAYC enjoys high customer retention and loyalty. More interestingly, PAYC keeps growing faster than its mature peers like Automatic Data Processing (ADP) and Paychex (PAYX).

BETI, or Better Employee Transaction Interface, is another key aspect that differentiates PAYC from its competitors. It allows employees to manage/review/approve their own payroll by integrating payroll into employee-self service, which can limit manual errors. In short, BETI makes PAYC's system more employee-driven and automated. This is contrary to many products, which are solely tied to HR use. Its uniqueness allows PAYC to increase market appeal and set a more strategic pricing.

What’s more important is that PAYC remains highly sustainable as shown by its robust liquidity position. Its cash level has remained stable in the past twelve quarters. At $532M today, its cash has generally increased since Q4 2023. It also remains adequate all its current liabilities excluding client funds obligations. With that, PAYC maintains zero borrowings. Allowing it to sustain its operating capacity, continue innovating its tech infrastructure, and cover dividends.

Peer Valuation

Relative to its peers, I believe that PAYC remains fairly valued. Using its EV/Revenue, PAYC appears to be trading at the lower end of the group. Yet, its revenue growth is the second highest on the list. This makes it cheap with decent growth. For EV/EBITDA, PAYC is still trading at a decent level, although much higher than ADP and PAYX. Note that PAYC has a higher EBITDA Margin. Despite this, its valuation appears justifiable considering its 31.9% EBITDA Margin. This means that for every $1 revenue, PAYC generates $0.32 of earnings, making it an efficient player on the list. Also, it passed the Rule of 40 along with PAYX. This means that PAYC maintains a strong balance between growth and profitability.

My Reservation: Why It’s Challenging To Expect A Stock Price Upside

We know that PAYC is a strong player considering its performance, liquidity, and reasonable valuation. However, the probability of it to rebound to its 2020-2022 highs may take some time. Why? In those years, we saw a huge expansion of HCM solutions demand. Lockdowns and revenge travel highlighted the need for having these solutions and services. They made it possible for employers to manage and monitor their employees whenever they wanted, and wherever they were. In my previous coverage, I explained that the work landscape continues to evolve today. From 100% remote work, it shifted to hybrid work.

Today, we know that a lot of companies are implementing return-to-office orders or RTOs. A survey in Q4 2024 revealed that over 30% of companies would force employees to return to office this year. As of this writing big companies like JPMorgan, Ford, Amazon are among those implementing RTOs. With that, there may be some demand or sales to lose soon.

On a lighter note, the majority of U.S. companies prefer to meet halfway by shifting to hybrid setups. So, much of HCM solutions demand will be retained. The thing is, I’m almost certain that PAYC will retain most of its customers considering its strong market and financial positioning. It can afford to set lower prices and expedite its tech infrastructure enhancements to raise its competitiveness.

My concern is that it will not be easy for HCM solution companies to raise their revenue growth back to their 2020-2022 boom. The same applies to PAYC. We can see how its revenue growth skyrocketed from 14.0% in 2020 to 27.3% in 2022 before weakening again to 18.2% in 2023, 10.8% in 2024. In the past six quarters, we have seen revenue growth weakening as well.

Despite this, PAYC may find new opportunities amid the AI revolution. It can leverage AI to further enhance its system. One aspect is that AI can be used to reduce other manual processes, such as talent management and time tracking. It can also use AI to generate predictive analytics to make new strategies in boosting engagement and increasing efficiency. With proper implementation, AI can increase its competitiveness and widen its margins in the long run. After all, its creation of BETI makes it a step ahead of many of its peers.

Using this information, I decided to update my DCF Model. I made no changes in its perpetual growth to be comparable to my previous analysis. I still derived its WACC using the Capital Asset Pricing Model. And for the net working capital, I still used the same process- accounts receivable using DSO, accounts payable using DPO, and the remaining operating assets and liabilities as a percentage of revenue.

The adjustments came from NOPAT, especially revenues. In my previous model, I used its five-year CAGR of 17.5% to project revenues in 2025-2029. However, I realized that it does fit the current situation. Instead, I based revenue growth on its quarterly trends since 2024 and derived 10.8%. I still set COGS and operating expenses as a percentage of revenue, so their values and NOPAT also changed. Since NOPAT has already changed after adjusting revenues, FCFF also changed. This led to a decrease in TP from $234.89 before to $210.17.

Technicals

PAYC made a huge price jump right after the Q2 2025 release. However, it is important to determine first if this sudden rebound will hold. Note that PAYC has shown early bearish signals recently. The 50-day SMA has shifted its direction recently, showing a less optimistic market sentiment. This is in line with the fact that the price broke below its $222 support last July 11. Of course, it is still too early to confirm a bearish reversal. After all, both the price and the 50-day SMA are still above the 200-day SMA.

Meanwhile, MACD is still in negative territory, showing that more bears are still in the market. The histogram went back to positive territory since it was pulled up by the price jump today. However, we can see its decreasing trend in the past two trading days, which shows recent sell-offs. On a lighter note, its RSI is still far below the overbought region. This shows that there is still some room for buying before profit-taking.

Conclusion

Paycom Software, Inc. remains a strong contender in the HCM solutions market today. Its revenue growth, stable margins, and robust liquidity position show its capacity to maintain a strong market positioning and sustain dividends. However, its toppish revenue growth suggests that HCM solutions had already reached their peak three years ago. This might make it hard for the stock price to return to its previous highs at this point. We must also consider its early bearish signals as we try to determine if the price jump will hold. I’m downgrading my rating from Buy to Hold.

data.title: Scottish Mortgage: Heavy Tech Exposure Making Me Nervous (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Scottish Mortgage Investment Trust (OTCPK:STMZF) has been a stellar performer during certain periods of its long history. But as it is a listed investment vehicle, understanding its current holdings is important when assessing the short- to medium-term opportunity the stock presents.

I last wrote about the name in my June 2024 “buy” piece, Scottish Mortgage: Still Value To Be Had. Since then it has moved up 24%.

I reckoned the trust’s strategy of investing in compelling growth stories remained sound. I still do, and from a long-term perspective, I continue to see this as an attractive stock. From a short- or medium-term perspective, though, I’m getting nervous about its tech exposure amid what I expect to be a period of high stock market volatility in coming months. For that basis, for now I am downgrading the shares to “hold”.

The Basic Challenge is a Mixed Blessing

On one hand, Scottish Mortgage’s strong long-term performance (it is up 295% over the past decade) reflects its heavy tech exposure (and more than that, its excellent track record in stock selection within that space).

My concern is that the tech market increasingly looks set for a fall at some point in coming months, in my opinion. Most people on SA have their own opinion on that, of course, and they span the waterfront. But a look at Scottish Mortgage’s current portfolio brings the question into sharp relief. The top ten at the moment are as follows:

Stock

%age of Scottish Mortgage’s portfolio

SpaceX

7.4%

MercadoLibre (MELI)

6.5%

Amazon (AMZN)

4.9%

Meta (META)

4.4%

Spotify (SPOT)

4.1%

TSMC (TSM)

4%

Bytedance (BDNCE)

3.5%

NVIDIA (NVDA)

2.9%

Ferrari (RACE)

2.9%

ASML (ASML)

2.8%

Those ten account for 43.4% of the portfolio. With the exception of Ferrari (with an $85bn market cap that looks racy to me even for the prancing horse) it’s all in big tech.

The full portfolio has further big tech exposure. So there’s a concentration risk here: Scottish Mortgage as it stands is a form of proxy for the stock market performance of big tech, for better (as we have seen over the past decade) or worse.

Valuation Looks Increasingly Exposed to Tech Valuation Risk

So the rub here is that, with its tech-heavy portfolio, Scottish Mortgage is exposed to a risk of share price fall if, say, an Autumn market correction drags down tech.

The shares currently sell for a discount to net asset value of 9%, arguably reflecting that risk, although in fairness it has been selling at a fairly steep discount (this is a FTSE 100 share we’re talking about here) for quite a while. That could also reflect other factors, such as the subjectivity of applying a valuation to an unlisted company such as Scottish Mortgage’s largest holding, in SpaceX.

The big question here, then, becomes how likely is it that the key tech stocks will move down (or crash) in coming months versus stay where they are or move up (which would be excellent news for Scottish Mortgage’s stock price)?

You pay your money, you take your choice.

I am nervous about what I see as high valuations for some of those companies that could fall sharply when what I see as the mounting (though contestable) evidence of U.S. economic weakness and already strong evidence of weakness in select other large economies gets properly factored into the stock market. That could happen (or not) at any point, but I would be somewhat surprised if we have not seen at least a correction by October/November, given the sizeable economic and geopolitical challenges currently on the table.

MercadoLibre trades at a forward P/E of 48, with Amazon at 32, and Meta at 28, according to SA (though the current P/Es, sourced from Google Finance, are even pricier at 60, 33, and 28, respectively). Massive expenditure on AI and data centers will eat into earnings at many of these companies.

In short, I think there is a fair risk of a sharp market downturn in the tech sector in the coming months. The mere fear of that could hurt Scottish Mortgage, and if it eventuates, it certainly will. There could still be short- to medium-term upside from here, but I see it as risky. Scottish Mortgage has rallied 68% since October 2023. The immediate future looks less compelling to me.

Long-term Approach Remains Attractive

Stepping back from that, though, and it is worth noting that from a long-term perspective, Scottish Mortgage’s investment approach continues to deliver the goods.

There was some concern when the longstanding trust manager retired in 2022 that the magic would fizzle out. But I do not think it has.

The current biggest holding is a star performer: SpaceX. A few years ago, that spot was held by Tesla (TSLA) but the trust has reduced its (enormously profitable) stake to the point that Tesla now accounts for less than 1% of the Scottish Mortgage portfolio.

Meanwhile, though the dividend yield is just 0.4%, the payout per share continues to grow annually. It was last cut in the aftermath of the 1929 crash. From a long-term perspective, I continue to have confidence in Scottish Mortgage’s strategy. But I reckon there could be bumps in the road over the next several months and do not currently see this as an attractive buying opportunity.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Snap-On Incorporated: Expensive With Limited Catalyst Visibility
data.description:
Snap-on Incorporated (NYSE:SNA) is a manufacturer and distributor of professional tools, diagnostic software, and repair solutions for automotive and industrial technicians through its proprietary direct-to-customer network. I initiate on SNA with a Sell rating, as SNA's 11.45x CY26e EV/EBITDA valuation multiple is at the 75th percentile of its 3-year range. Using my mean reversion approach to valuation, SNA's 0.44x premium to its historical average multiple is fully rolled off by CY26e, supporting my $218.39 target price and -31% downside potential. SNA's Tools Group shift into 'quick-payback' hand and power tools should generate incremental sales/mix, albeit in a tough macro environment, which supports my CY26e EBITDA forecast of $1.012 billion (21.0% margin). However, significant pricing pressure from value competitors as well as technician budget constraints will likely limit Snap-on's margin opportunity. Therefore, I believe that with Tools Group execution largely modeled in and without a step up in new vehicle or technician demand or improvement opportunities, Snap-on's shares have limited further multiple upside.

RS&I Intelligent Diagnostics Drives Recurring Revenue and Margin Upside

With increasing EV and ADAS complexity driving the need for more sophisticated vehicle diagnostics, I expect SNA's RS&I segment to provide a catalyst in the coming months as it grows the APOLLO+ and Triton intelligent diagnostic systems business. As SNA has discussed this new strategic initiative in its recent earnings calls and investor events, I expect management to begin commenting on initial traction on those products in its upcoming earnings calls.

I also think SNA will highlight the early success of its subscription attach rate, which increases its addressable market to DIY customers and non-typical auto repair shops, and overall subscription units, which move RS&I's revenue mix toward higher-margin recurring revenue. Investors will look for metrics around the product placements and subscription attach rates as early indicators to help model the embedded growth opportunity.

Given the potential for SNA to point to tangible momentum indicators in RS&I, I believe there is an element of positive surprise around RS&I revenue, subscription revenue, and EBITDA growth in the coming quarters. This is particularly relevant given the headwinds experienced by the Tools group in recent quarters, as I believe incremental software subscription revenue could help offset margin pressure as technicians are under increasing pressure to purchase more value-oriented tools in their constrained budgets.

If SNA is able to demonstrate strong momentum in increasing subscription rates and other software sales metrics, I believe this could point to performance that comes in above current consensus estimates in RS&I revenue and EBITDA, leading to upside to my current estimates. This, in turn, could result in slightly offsetting the current downturn in Tools segment EBITDA caused by pricing pressures, potentially leading to an upside for overall segment profitability.

With analysts consequently likely to raise estimates based on this momentum, this could drive a re-rating of SNA's EV/EBITDA multiple toward its historical average as investors react positively to the recurring revenue story, providing the catalyst to close SNA's valuation discount to its historical average.

Quick-Payback Tool Adoption Is The Key Focus For Near-Term Sales/Margins Upside

SNA's pivot to quick-payback tools should continue to be a key sales attributer over the next few months. I expect Q3 results to shed more light on the adoption of quick payback tools among 'cash rich but confidence poor' technicians.

While I anticipate the commentary to provide reasons for optimism on the outlook for the Tools group, I believe investors will want to pay attention to the year-over-hand tool/power tool category growth rates in proprietary van, diagnostic tool attach rates and feedback around more frequent purchase decision-making on the quick payback tools and lower priced alternatives (Craftsman, Gearwrench, Tekton, etc.).

Furthermore, I expect management to address whether the improving product mix and technician feedback indicate that Snap-on can sustain premium pricing in exchange for demonstrable productivity benefits.

Bottom line: I expect sequential tools revenue to have accelerated 3–5% QoQ, given the uplift from quick payback tools, which can drive 20–30bps of incremental tools margin. SNA is leveraging its fixed costs vs. increasing volume through quick payback tools.

A better than expected performance driven by quick payback tool momentum and SNA convincing the market that its premium pricing is stickier or the market should expect to see benefits from SNA's better pricing ability would be positive for SNA vs my CY26e largely stable EBITDA numbers ($1,012m, 21.0% margins).

Finally SNA's ability to convince the market that it has the tools that appeal to the D2T technicians primarily towards the productivity improvements would be key for SNA's qualitative story around D2T, and I believe it would be a driver of multiple expansion.

Commercial & Industrial Custom-Torque & Kit Momentum To Support Segment Sustainability

Snap-on's focus on specialty tools and kits for Aerospace, Military and Energy customers paves the way for in-line mid-single-digit growth in C&I organically likely over the next 12–24 months. In these markets, precise application and traceability are extremely important, and SNA's turnkey kits and solutions with digital reporting embedded address exactly that.

Investors should be on the lookout for C&I order book expansion, commentary on new programme launches and ASP trends on future earnings calls as leading indicators of C&I traction. I expect this catalyst to drive about ~25–50bps of margin tailwinds through mix uplift following higher-value product bundles, helping to offset the pricing pressure Tools Group has experienced the last 1–2 years.

Although a longer cycle nature of sales may introduce some volatility here, a sustained cadence of new project wins in Aerospace and D&E will validate SNA's moat and de-risk my CY26e EBITDA margins (21.0%) marginally.

While I note growth in C&I can serve as a valuable stabilizer for the near term, in my view, absent a broader improvement in tech demand, this is unlikely to drive multiple expansion in my thesis for a Sell.

What Could Drive The Stock Even Lower?

My investment thesis on Snap-on is predicated on the mix shift into higher-margin diagnostic software and the WC-driven FCF tailwind that support the 11.0x EV/EBITDA valuation. Which I think is already expensive. I've identified three company-specific risks that could threaten the multiple to go even lower:

First, a slowdown in vehicle repair activity that could result from slowing miles driven, fast EV penetration (lower need for repairs), or slower ADAS/electrification development could pressure software penetration and, in turn, the 100 bps mix shift that I model, which drives 50 bps of margin expansion.

Second credit stress among its franchisees and loss of route could threaten its cash-flow execution. Underperforming dealers could defer purchases or could default on loans, increasing the risk of credit-loss provisions, rising DSO, and loss of the ~£100m annual WC tailwind I model for CY26.

Third competition from low-cost tool manufacturers and/or underperformance of its new hand- and power-tool launches could pressure productivity and pricing power, threatening my 21.0% CY26e EBITDA margin and exposing SNA to multiple compression.

Risks To My Sell Thesis

We sort of have to think about everything in reverse here. Improved traction vehicle repair could drive multiple expansion and be fundamentally a problem to my sell thesis. Overall improvements and easing by the Fed can cause general expansion in multiples that, again, without substantial underlying business performance improvement, cause havoc to my rating.

Overall investors closely watch the factors mentioned in what could drive the stock lower. As both upside and downside potential to this thesis.

Conclusion

I'm bearish as Snap-on's CY26e EV/EBITDA multiple (11.45x) would sit at a multiyear high with limited catalyst visibility. At the core of my bearish thesis is the idea that rising software margins from mix shift into higher-margin diagnostics and quick-payback tools are already priced into the stock. What I will be looking for: RS&I subscription attach rates as well as broader technician demand indicators for signs of upside or broader levels of downside.

data.title: Nebius Group: The New AI Champion
data.description:
In early July, we issued a second public Buy rating on Nebius Group N.V. (NASDAQ:NBIS) at $46. Shares are up 46% from there at the time of this writing. Of course, today shares are up about 20% following a well-received Q2 earnings report.

We continue to view Nebius as a long-term winner. Right now a lot of traditional valuation metrics do not make sense to use here, so, given it is a hypergrowth name, we are looking for more revenue, more customers, and moves more in the direction toward less cash burn, to translate to more gains. You see this a lot with growth tech companies. This can continue for a few more quarters, but at some point the market will want to see a move to true profit for this company with its huge focus on AI infrastructure.

Right now, a lot of AI infrastructure plays are popular with the market and we see that continuing, outside some possible seasonal weakness. This company recognizes the need for large-scale GPUs and cloud platforms while having strategic partner businesses working on generative AI development, tech career skill building, and autonomous vehicle tech. The Street is upbeat. In this article, we update on the hypergrowth name. Let us discuss.

Q2 results were strong, big recurring revenue

In the just reported Q2 earnings, Nebius reported revenue of $105 million, a huge sequential increase from the $55 million in Q1. Further, it increased by an incredible 625% year-over-year. This was a beat of $4 million against estimates. Just huge growth. This is why we call it 'hypergrowth.' Such revenue growth stemmed from investments in its core AI infrastructure business. Now we have annualized recurring revenue pushing $1 billion. This is huge for the company, and was something we had opined was in the cards for the company. The guide for the year was $900 million to $1.1 billion. To be clear and balanced, if it comes in on the low end, this could be taken as a negative, so, we are looking for $1 billion plus to keep the stock rally going in the medium-term.

High costs, losses from operations

While we continue to see huge growth in revenue, it is worth noting that this comes with huge increases in operating costs and revenues. This is one negative to be aware of. And shorts are betting against the company. But that is part of why news like today's earnings results in big rallies. However, we are seeing one positive here, and that is the there was swing to positive net income this quarter which was huge, and the loss from operations was actually $1 million less than a year ago. Year to date, loss from operations is about $50 million higher than the comparable period a year ago, but we are seeing positive news.

In Q2, the cost of revenue was up to $30.1 million from $7.7million last year. The product development costs were $42.8 million, rising from $32.0 million. Sales and admin expenses were however well controlled at to $68.2 million, down from $75.6 million last year. There as some big depreciation costs, up to $75.2 million, versus $11.4 million last year. And so, total operating costs and expenses nearly doubled from last year to $216.3 million, so, there was a $111.2 million loss from operations. But this was down $1 million from last year.

Generally speaking, Nebius loses some serious money. However, down line on the income from operations statement, we saw a gain from a revaluation of its equity investments. That was good for $597.4 million. So that led to positive net income of $584.4 million. Of course adjusting for this and other expenses, we saw adjusted EBITDA loss of $21.0 million. The good news? This was down 64% from a year ago. But adjusted net loss increased 49% to $91.5 million. So, it is losing more money now than it was a year ago. Eventually, the market will care about this. For now, it is about growing those revenues.

Cash is in good shape right now, but still burning cash

Cash and equivalents at the end of the quarter were $1.67 billion, down $755 million from the start of the year. Remember the company took in $2.5 billion from a divestment last year and got a $700 million investment total from high-level investors a few months ago. But it is still burning cash. OF course, cash was up from Q1, but that was because they entered in another round of debt. In June, the company entered into an agreement for the private placement of senior unsecured convertible notes, totaling $1 billion, with two tranches, both $500 million each.

So, Nebius is burning money to build out its AI business, and is actively exploring new sites. Keep in mind again that it went from having just one location in Finland, to now five in Europe, the United States, and the Middle East, with plans for much more expansion. This is positive.

Looking ahead

We still have a hypergrowth name here, and it is all about revenues, and the annualized recurring revenue. We got a strong outlook for $1 billion on this metrics at the midpoint. This has been seen as bullish.

The revenue growth is outpacing the growth of operating expenses, which is positive, but the company is still losing money and burning cash. So, it has a few quarters of cash to burn, but likely will issue more debt in early 2026, something to be aware of, just given how much the company is investing in its growth. Overall, it is tough to chase Nebius Group N.V. stock here, but we remain bullish on the long term.

data.title: Elanco Animal Health Q2: Rising Innovation Revenue, Initiate At Buy
data.description:
Elanco Animal Health Incorporated (NYSE:ELAN) develops drugs to prevent and treat disease in farm animals and pets, competing with Zoetis (ZTS), the industry leader in animal pharmaceuticals. I believe the company’s focus on innovative products could potentially diversify its product categories in both companion and farm animal markets. Elanco posted a strong quarterly result and raised its full-year guidance. I am initiating with a Buy rating with a fair value of $20 per share.

Similar to Zoetis, Elanco develops and manufactures parasiticides, vaccines and therapeutics for companion animals, and medicated feed additives, injectable antibiotics and vaccines for farm animals. Due to its small scale, the company has focused on select innovative products such as Zenrelia and Credelio Quattro, as shown in the slide below. For instance, Elanco received FDA clearance for Zenrelia, a JAK inhibitor targeting control of pruritus and atopic dermatitis in dogs, in 2024, as noted in its 10K. The Zenrelia market share has increased form only 2% this March to 4% in June, with more than 10,000 clinics starting to use this products. As an animal pharmaceutical company, innovative drug launches are crucial to its future growth, in my view.

Elanco has strategically divested non-core business to improve its capital allocation and focus on pet health and livestock. On July 9th, 2024, the company divested its aqua business to Merck Animal Health for $1.294 billion. The company used the proceeds to pay down its debt. As the aqua business provides products for warm-water and cold-water species, there are no synergies between its aqua business and companion and farm animals market. I view the divestiture makes strategic sense.

Elanco released its Q2 FY25 result on August 7th, posting 8% organic revenue growth with $0.26 of adjusted EPS. As detailed in the table below, the company delivered both price and volume growth for the quarter, across both pet health and farm animal segments.

I think it is impressive to see the management raised its innovation revenue target to $720 - $800 million for the full year, representing around 17% of total revenues. The innovation business is very important for the company to turn around its overall organic growth and gain market share in both companion and farm animal spaces. As depicted in the slide below, the company has gradually improved its innovations over the past few years, a strategic move to reaccelerate its overall growth.

Due to its strong quarterly result, the company raised its full-year guidance as detailed in the slide below:

Business Research Insights forecasts the global animal pharmaceutical market will grow at a CAGR of 5.13% from 2025 to 2033, driven by rising awareness of animal health and well-being. With more innovative drug launches, I think Elanco can at least grow in line with the overall market. For FY25, I forecast the company will grow its revenue by 6%, in line with its guidance. From FY26 onwards, I anticipate a 7.2% total revenue growth, assuming 5% organic growth and 2.2% M&A growth. I estimate the company could spend 3% of revenue on M&A, contributing to an additional 2.2% growth to the topline.

Elanco has been actively restructuring its operations to reduce overhead costs and optimize its global supply chain. They have spent a total of $460 million on restructurings over the past three years, which could support a margin improvement in the near future. I forecast the company will deliver 50bps annual margin improvement assuming 20bps from new products mix; 20bps from reduction in SG&A and 10bps from operating leverage of sales and marketing expenses. I calculate the total operating costs will grow by 6.6% annually.

The DCF can be summarized as follows:

The FCFF is calculated below:

The WACC is estimated to be 8.1% assuming risk free 4.2%; beta 1.1; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $7 billion; debt $4.3 billion; tax rate 25%. I set the terminal growth rate to be 4%, assuming 2% pricing growth and 2% volume growth.

Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting net debt, the fair value is calculated to be $20 per share, as per my estimate.

Key Risk

As shown in the slide below, Elanco exited the quarter with 4x net debt leverage, a pretty high level in my view. While the company plans to reduce it leverage below 3x over time, the company’s high debt level would limit its capacity for shares buyback and strategic acquisitions.

As noted in its 10K, Elanco has customer concentration risks, with a third-party veterinary distributor, an affiliate of Cencora, representing 11% of total revenues. If the distributor decides to switch to anther manufacturer, Elanco’s growth will be significantly affected.

Lastly, the company has $4.4 billion of goodwill on its balance sheet, which could face future impairment charges. For instance, the company charged $1.042 billion to goodwill and intangible impairment in FY23 due to rising interest rate. The amount of goodwill is quite significant compared to its market cap of $7.05 billion.

Conclusion

Elanco Animal Health is in the process of launching more innovative products in both companion and farm animal markets, which could potentially accelerate its overall revenue growth. I am initiating with a Buy rating with a fair value of $20 per share.
